In [38]:
lfw_pca_120 = PCA(n_components=120)
x_train_pca_120 = lfw_pca_120.fit_transform(x_train)
x_test_pca_120 = lfw_pca_120.transform(x_test)
In [39]:
clf_svm_120 = svm.SVC(C=10,kernel='rbf', gamma='scale', decision_function_shape='ovo')
clf_svm_120.fit(x_train_pca_120, y_train)
Out[39]:
SVC(C=10, decision_function_shape='ovo')
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
C C: float, default=1.0

Regularization parameter. The strength of the regularization is
inversely proportional to C. Must be strictly positive. The penalty
is a squared l2 penalty. For an intuitive visualization of the effects
of scaling the regularization parameter C, see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_scale_c.py`. 		10
kernel kernel: {'linear', 'poly', 'rbf', 'sigmoid', 'precomputed'} or callable, default='rbf'

Specifies the kernel type to be used in the algorithm. If
none is given, 'rbf' will be used. If a callable is given it is used to
pre-compute the kernel matrix from data matrices; that matrix should be
an array of shape ``(n_samples, n_samples)``. For an intuitive
visualization of different kernel types see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_kernels.py`. 		'rbf'
degree degree: int, default=3

Degree of the polynomial kernel function ('poly').
Must be non-negative. Ignored by all other kernels. 		3
gamma gamma: {'scale', 'auto'} or float, default='scale'

Kernel coefficient for 'rbf', 'poly' and 'sigmoid'.

- if ``gamma='scale'`` (default) is passed then it uses
1 / (n_features * X.var()) as value of gamma,
- if 'auto', uses 1 / n_features
- if float, must be non-negative.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``gamma`` changed from 'auto' to 'scale'. 		'scale'
coef0 coef0: float, default=0.0

Independent term in kernel function.
It is only significant in 'poly' and 'sigmoid'. 		0.0
shrinking shrinking: bool, default=True

Whether to use the shrinking heuristic.
See the :ref:`User Guide `. 		True
probability probability: bool, default=False

Whether to enable probability estimates. This must be enabled prior
to calling `fit`, will slow down that method as it internally uses
5-fold cross-validation, and `predict_proba` may be inconsistent with
`predict`. Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `. 		False
tol tol: float, default=1e-3

Tolerance for stopping criterion. 		0.001
cache_size cache_size: float, default=200

Specify the size of the kernel cache (in MB). 		200
class_weight class_weight: dict or 'balanced', default=None

Set the parameter C of class i to class_weight[i]*C for
SVC. If not given, all classes are supposed to have
weight one.
The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``. 		None
verbose verbose: bool, default=False

Enable verbose output. Note that this setting takes advantage of a
per-process runtime setting in libsvm that, if enabled, may not work
properly in a multithreaded context. 		False
max_iter max_iter: int, default=-1

Hard limit on iterations within solver, or -1 for no limit. 		-1
decision_function_shape decision_function_shape: {'ovo', 'ovr'}, default='ovr'

Whether to return a one-vs-rest ('ovr') decision function of shape
(n_samples, n_classes) as all other classifiers, or the original
one-vs-one ('ovo') decision function of libsvm which has shape
(n_samples, n_classes * (n_classes - 1) / 2). However, note that
internally, one-vs-one ('ovo') is always used as a multi-class strategy
to train models; an ovr matrix is only constructed from the ovo matrix.
The parameter is ignored for binary classification.

.. versionchanged:: 0.19
decision_function_shape is 'ovr' by default.

.. versionadded:: 0.17
*decision_function_shape='ovr'* is recommended.

.. versionchanged:: 0.17
Deprecated *decision_function_shape='ovo' and None*. 		'ovo'
break_ties break_ties: bool, default=False

If true, ``decision_function_shape='ovr'``, and number of classes > 2,
:term:`predict` will break ties according to the confidence values of
:term:`decision_function`; otherwise the first class among the tied
classes is returned. Please note that breaking ties comes at a
relatively high computational cost compared to a simple predict. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_tie_breaking.py` for an
example of its usage with ``decision_function_shape='ovr'``.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		False
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls the pseudo random number generation for shuffling the data for
probability estimates. Ignored when `probability` is False.
Pass an int for reproducible output across multiple function calls.
See :term:`Glossary `. 		None
In [40]:
clf_rf_120  = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    max_features='sqrt',
    class_weight='balanced',
    random_state=42
)
clf_rf_120.fit(x_train_pca_120, y_train)
Out[40]:
RandomForestClassifier(class_weight='balanced', n_estimators=200,
                       random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		200
criterion criterion: {"gini", "entropy", "log_loss"}, default="gini"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria are
"gini" for the Gini impurity and "log_loss" and "entropy" both for the
Shannon information gain, see :ref:`tree_mathematical_formulation`.
Note: This parameter is tree-specific. 		'gini'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		None
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default="sqrt"

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to `"sqrt"`.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		'sqrt'
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		None
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
class_weight class_weight: {"balanced", "balanced_subsample"}, dict or list of dicts, default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If not given, all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems, a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example,
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 5}, {0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 1}] instead of
[{1:1}, {2:5}, {3:1}, {4:1}].

The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

The "balanced_subsample" mode is the same as "balanced" except that
weights are computed based on the bootstrap sample for every tree
grown.

For multi-output, the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified. 		'balanced'
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonic increase
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonic decrease

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multiclass classifications (i.e. when `n_classes > 2`),
- multioutput classifications (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- classifications trained on data with missing values.

The constraints hold over the probability of the positive class.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None
In [41]:
print('------------------SVM Classifier metrics------------------')
evaluate_model(clf_svm_120, x_test_pca_120, y_test)

------------------SVM Classifier metrics------------------
                   precision    recall  f1-score   support

     Ariel Sharon       0.78      0.67      0.72        21
     Colin Powell       0.83      0.91      0.87        77
  Donald Rumsfeld       0.54      0.66      0.59        32
    George W Bush       0.81      0.90      0.85       162
Gerhard Schroeder       0.60      0.60      0.60        30
      Hugo Chavez       0.92      0.52      0.67        21
   Jacques Chirac       0.83      0.29      0.43        17
    Jean Chretien       0.82      0.82      0.82        17
    John Ashcroft       1.00      0.84      0.91        19
Junichiro Koizumi       0.92      0.85      0.88        13
  Serena Williams       1.00      0.85      0.92        13
       Tony Blair       0.77      0.74      0.76        46

         accuracy                           0.79       468
        macro avg       0.82      0.72      0.75       468
     weighted avg       0.80      0.79      0.79       468
In [42]:
print('------------------RF Classifier metrics------------------')
evaluate_model(clf_rf_120, x_test_pca_120, y_test)

------------------RF Classifier metrics------------------
                   precision    recall  f1-score   support

     Ariel Sharon       1.00      0.05      0.09        21
     Colin Powell       0.96      0.31      0.47        77
  Donald Rumsfeld       1.00      0.06      0.12        32
    George W Bush       0.38      1.00      0.55       162
Gerhard Schroeder       1.00      0.03      0.06        30
      Hugo Chavez       0.00      0.00      0.00        21
   Jacques Chirac       0.00      0.00      0.00        17
    Jean Chretien       0.00      0.00      0.00        17
    John Ashcroft       0.00      0.00      0.00        19
Junichiro Koizumi       1.00      0.31      0.47        13
  Serena Williams       1.00      0.54      0.70        13
       Tony Blair       1.00      0.07      0.12        46

         accuracy                           0.44       468
        macro avg       0.61      0.20      0.22       468
     weighted avg       0.62      0.44      0.33       468

Najlepsze wyniki uzyskano dla klasyfikatora SVM przy redukcji wymiarowości do 120 komponentów PCA (accuracy = 0.79, macro F1 = 0.75)

Random Forest osiągnął istotnie gorsze wyniki, szczególnie dla większej liczby komponentów. Najlepsze rezultaty uzyskano przy redukcji do 50 wymiarów (accuracy = 0.52).

Wyniki wskazują, że SVM lepiej radzi sobie z danymi obrazowymi reprezentowanymi w przestrzeni PCA, podczas gdy Random Forest ma ograniczoną zdolność do modelowania złożonych zależności w tym zadaniu

In [43]:
eigenfaces = lfw_pca.components_

h, w = lfw.images.shape[1:3]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 5, figsize=(10, 8))

for i, ax in enumerate(axes.flat):
    ax.imshow(eigenfaces[i].reshape(h, w), cmap='gray')
    ax.set_title(f"PC {i+1}")
    ax.axis('off')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Eigenfaces można rozumieć jako zestaw „podstawowych wzorców twarzy”, któreopisują najważniejsze różnice między obrazami w zbiorze danych. Każda twarz może być przedstawiona jako kombinacja tych wzorców.W przypadku nowego, wcześniej niewidzianego zdjęcia, zamiast analizować wszystkie piksele, obraz jest przekształcany do nowej przestrzeni cech utworzonej przez eigenfaces. W efekcie otrzymujemy krótszy wektor liczb, który mówi, w jakim stopniu dane wzorce (eigenfaces) występują w tym obrazie.

Pierwsze komponenty główne opisują globalne cechy, takie jak oświetlenie i ogólny kształt twarzy. Kolejne komponenty uchwytują bardziej szczegółowe elementy, takie jak oczy, nos i usta, natomiast dalsze odpowiadają za drobne detale i szum.

Zadanie dodatkowe: Kernel PCA¶

In [44]:
N = 1000
r1 = 40
r2 = 100
sigma = 1

n_half = N // 2

theta = np.random.uniform(0, np.pi, N)
phi = np.random.uniform(0, 2*np.pi, N)

r = np.concatenate([np.full(n_half, r1), np.full(n_half, r2)])


x = r * np.sin(theta) * np.cos(phi)
y = r * np.sin(theta) * np.sin(phi)
z = r * np.cos(theta)

x += np.random.normal(0, sigma, N)
y += np.random.normal(0, sigma, N)
z += np.random.normal(0, sigma, N)

X = np.vstack([x, y, z]).T
y_labels = np.array([0]*n_half + [1]*n_half)
In [45]:
standard_pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_transformed_pca = standard_pca.fit_transform(X)

print(standard_pca.explained_variance_ratio_)

[0.5131938  0.26166831]
In [46]:
kernel_pca = KernelPCA(n_components=2, kernel='poly', degree=5)
X_transformed_kernel_pca = kernel_pca.fit_transform(X)
print(kernel_pca.eigenvectors_)

[[ 0.00069613 -0.00024477]
 [ 0.00010039 -0.00049841]
 [ 0.00030854  0.00017178]
 ...
 [ 0.07776337 -0.01736889]
 [ 0.03098372 -0.06929237]
 [-0.02526445  0.03659353]]
In [47]:
kernel_pca_rbf = KernelPCA(n_components=2, kernel='rbf', gamma=1/(2*27.8**2))
X_transformed_kernel_pca_rbf = kernel_pca_rbf.fit_transform(X)
In [48]:
def plot(X_transformed, title):
    plt.scatter(X_transformed[:,0], X_transformed[:,1], c=y_labels)
    plt.title(title)
    plt.show()

plot(X_transformed_pca, "PCA")
plot(X_transformed_kernel_pca, "Kernel PCA - poly")
plot(X_transformed_kernel_pca_rbf , "Kernel PCA - RBF")

Jak możemy zauważyć na powyższych wykresach punkty reprezentujace powierzchnie sfer, zostały wpełni odseparowane dopiero, podczas zastosowania modelu Kernel PCA z jądrem typy RBF.

In [49]:
kernel_pca_rbf = KernelPCA(n_components=100, kernel='rbf')

x_train_pca = kernel_pca_rbf.fit_transform(x_train)
x_test_pca = kernel_pca_rbf.transform(x_test)
In [50]:
clf_svm = svm.SVC(kernel='linear', C=10, gamma='scale', class_weight='balanced', decision_function_shape='ovo')
clf_svm.fit(x_train_pca, y_train)
Out[50]:
SVC(C=10, class_weight='balanced', decision_function_shape='ovo',
    kernel='linear')
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
C C: float, default=1.0

Regularization parameter. The strength of the regularization is
inversely proportional to C. Must be strictly positive. The penalty
is a squared l2 penalty. For an intuitive visualization of the effects
of scaling the regularization parameter C, see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_scale_c.py`. 		10
kernel kernel: {'linear', 'poly', 'rbf', 'sigmoid', 'precomputed'} or callable, default='rbf'

Specifies the kernel type to be used in the algorithm. If
none is given, 'rbf' will be used. If a callable is given it is used to
pre-compute the kernel matrix from data matrices; that matrix should be
an array of shape ``(n_samples, n_samples)``. For an intuitive
visualization of different kernel types see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_kernels.py`. 		'linear'
degree degree: int, default=3

Degree of the polynomial kernel function ('poly').
Must be non-negative. Ignored by all other kernels. 		3
gamma gamma: {'scale', 'auto'} or float, default='scale'

Kernel coefficient for 'rbf', 'poly' and 'sigmoid'.

- if ``gamma='scale'`` (default) is passed then it uses
1 / (n_features * X.var()) as value of gamma,
- if 'auto', uses 1 / n_features
- if float, must be non-negative.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``gamma`` changed from 'auto' to 'scale'. 		'scale'
coef0 coef0: float, default=0.0

Independent term in kernel function.
It is only significant in 'poly' and 'sigmoid'. 		0.0
shrinking shrinking: bool, default=True

Whether to use the shrinking heuristic.
See the :ref:`User Guide `. 		True
probability probability: bool, default=False

Whether to enable probability estimates. This must be enabled prior
to calling `fit`, will slow down that method as it internally uses
5-fold cross-validation, and `predict_proba` may be inconsistent with
`predict`. Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `. 		False
tol tol: float, default=1e-3

Tolerance for stopping criterion. 		0.001
cache_size cache_size: float, default=200

Specify the size of the kernel cache (in MB). 		200
class_weight class_weight: dict or 'balanced', default=None

Set the parameter C of class i to class_weight[i]*C for
SVC. If not given, all classes are supposed to have
weight one.
The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``. 		'balanced'
verbose verbose: bool, default=False

Enable verbose output. Note that this setting takes advantage of a
per-process runtime setting in libsvm that, if enabled, may not work
properly in a multithreaded context. 		False
max_iter max_iter: int, default=-1

Hard limit on iterations within solver, or -1 for no limit. 		-1
decision_function_shape decision_function_shape: {'ovo', 'ovr'}, default='ovr'

Whether to return a one-vs-rest ('ovr') decision function of shape
(n_samples, n_classes) as all other classifiers, or the original
one-vs-one ('ovo') decision function of libsvm which has shape
(n_samples, n_classes * (n_classes - 1) / 2). However, note that
internally, one-vs-one ('ovo') is always used as a multi-class strategy
to train models; an ovr matrix is only constructed from the ovo matrix.
The parameter is ignored for binary classification.

.. versionchanged:: 0.19
decision_function_shape is 'ovr' by default.

.. versionadded:: 0.17
*decision_function_shape='ovr'* is recommended.

.. versionchanged:: 0.17
Deprecated *decision_function_shape='ovo' and None*. 		'ovo'
break_ties break_ties: bool, default=False

If true, ``decision_function_shape='ovr'``, and number of classes > 2,
:term:`predict` will break ties according to the confidence values of
:term:`decision_function`; otherwise the first class among the tied
classes is returned. Please note that breaking ties comes at a
relatively high computational cost compared to a simple predict. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_tie_breaking.py` for an
example of its usage with ``decision_function_shape='ovr'``.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		False
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls the pseudo random number generation for shuffling the data for
probability estimates. Ignored when `probability` is False.
Pass an int for reproducible output across multiple function calls.
See :term:`Glossary `. 		None
In [51]:
clf_rf  = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, max_depth=20, max_features='sqrt',class_weight='balanced', random_state=42)
clf_rf.fit(x_train_pca, y_train)
Out[51]:
RandomForestClassifier(class_weight='balanced', max_depth=20, n_estimators=200,
                       random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		200
criterion criterion: {"gini", "entropy", "log_loss"}, default="gini"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria are
"gini" for the Gini impurity and "log_loss" and "entropy" both for the
Shannon information gain, see :ref:`tree_mathematical_formulation`.
Note: This parameter is tree-specific. 		'gini'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		20
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default="sqrt"

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to `"sqrt"`.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		'sqrt'
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		None
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
class_weight class_weight: {"balanced", "balanced_subsample"}, dict or list of dicts, default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If not given, all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems, a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example,
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 5}, {0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 1}] instead of
[{1:1}, {2:5}, {3:1}, {4:1}].

The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

The "balanced_subsample" mode is the same as "balanced" except that
weights are computed based on the bootstrap sample for every tree
grown.

For multi-output, the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified. 		'balanced'
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonic increase
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonic decrease

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multiclass classifications (i.e. when `n_classes > 2`),
- multioutput classifications (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- classifications trained on data with missing values.

The constraints hold over the probability of the positive class.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None
In [52]:
print('------------------SVM Classifier metrics------------------')
evaluate_model(clf_svm, x_test_pca, y_test)

------------------SVM Classifier metrics------------------
                   precision    recall  f1-score   support

     Ariel Sharon       0.50      0.48      0.49        21
     Colin Powell       0.84      0.77      0.80        77
  Donald Rumsfeld       0.66      0.72      0.69        32
    George W Bush       0.93      0.64      0.76       162
Gerhard Schroeder       0.55      0.53      0.54        30
      Hugo Chavez       0.61      0.81      0.69        21
   Jacques Chirac       0.39      0.65      0.49        17
    Jean Chretien       0.52      0.88      0.65        17
    John Ashcroft       0.46      0.89      0.61        19
Junichiro Koizumi       0.69      0.85      0.76        13
  Serena Williams       0.50      0.92      0.65        13
       Tony Blair       0.68      0.59      0.63        46

         accuracy                           0.69       468
        macro avg       0.61      0.73      0.65       468
     weighted avg       0.74      0.69      0.70       468
In [53]:
print('------------------RF Classifier metrics------------------')
evaluate_model(clf_rf, x_test_pca, y_test)

------------------RF Classifier metrics------------------
                   precision    recall  f1-score   support

     Ariel Sharon       1.00      0.05      0.09        21
     Colin Powell       1.00      0.35      0.52        77
  Donald Rumsfeld       1.00      0.12      0.22        32
    George W Bush       0.39      1.00      0.56       162
Gerhard Schroeder       0.00      0.00      0.00        30
      Hugo Chavez       1.00      0.05      0.09        21
   Jacques Chirac       0.00      0.00      0.00        17
    Jean Chretien       1.00      0.06      0.11        17
    John Ashcroft       0.00      0.00      0.00        19
Junichiro Koizumi       1.00      0.31      0.47        13
  Serena Williams       1.00      0.77      0.87        13
       Tony Blair       1.00      0.04      0.08        46

         accuracy                           0.45       468
        macro avg       0.70      0.23      0.25       468
     weighted avg       0.65      0.45      0.35       468
In [54]:
kernel_pca_rbf_120 = KernelPCA(n_components=120, kernel='rbf')
x_train_120 = kernel_pca_rbf_120.fit_transform(x_train)
x_test_120 =  kernel_pca_rbf_120.transform(x_test)
In [55]:
clf_svm = svm.SVC(kernel='linear', C=10, gamma='scale', class_weight='balanced', decision_function_shape='ovo')
clf_svm.fit(x_train_120, y_train)
Out[55]:
SVC(C=10, class_weight='balanced', decision_function_shape='ovo',
    kernel='linear')
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
C C: float, default=1.0

Regularization parameter. The strength of the regularization is
inversely proportional to C. Must be strictly positive. The penalty
is a squared l2 penalty. For an intuitive visualization of the effects
of scaling the regularization parameter C, see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_scale_c.py`. 		10
kernel kernel: {'linear', 'poly', 'rbf', 'sigmoid', 'precomputed'} or callable, default='rbf'

Specifies the kernel type to be used in the algorithm. If
none is given, 'rbf' will be used. If a callable is given it is used to
pre-compute the kernel matrix from data matrices; that matrix should be
an array of shape ``(n_samples, n_samples)``. For an intuitive
visualization of different kernel types see
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_kernels.py`. 		'linear'
degree degree: int, default=3

Degree of the polynomial kernel function ('poly').
Must be non-negative. Ignored by all other kernels. 		3
gamma gamma: {'scale', 'auto'} or float, default='scale'

Kernel coefficient for 'rbf', 'poly' and 'sigmoid'.

- if ``gamma='scale'`` (default) is passed then it uses
1 / (n_features * X.var()) as value of gamma,
- if 'auto', uses 1 / n_features
- if float, must be non-negative.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``gamma`` changed from 'auto' to 'scale'. 		'scale'
coef0 coef0: float, default=0.0

Independent term in kernel function.
It is only significant in 'poly' and 'sigmoid'. 		0.0
shrinking shrinking: bool, default=True

Whether to use the shrinking heuristic.
See the :ref:`User Guide `. 		True
probability probability: bool, default=False

Whether to enable probability estimates. This must be enabled prior
to calling `fit`, will slow down that method as it internally uses
5-fold cross-validation, and `predict_proba` may be inconsistent with
`predict`. Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `. 		False
tol tol: float, default=1e-3

Tolerance for stopping criterion. 		0.001
cache_size cache_size: float, default=200

Specify the size of the kernel cache (in MB). 		200
class_weight class_weight: dict or 'balanced', default=None

Set the parameter C of class i to class_weight[i]*C for
SVC. If not given, all classes are supposed to have
weight one.
The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``. 		'balanced'
verbose verbose: bool, default=False

Enable verbose output. Note that this setting takes advantage of a
per-process runtime setting in libsvm that, if enabled, may not work
properly in a multithreaded context. 		False
max_iter max_iter: int, default=-1

Hard limit on iterations within solver, or -1 for no limit. 		-1
decision_function_shape decision_function_shape: {'ovo', 'ovr'}, default='ovr'

Whether to return a one-vs-rest ('ovr') decision function of shape
(n_samples, n_classes) as all other classifiers, or the original
one-vs-one ('ovo') decision function of libsvm which has shape
(n_samples, n_classes * (n_classes - 1) / 2). However, note that
internally, one-vs-one ('ovo') is always used as a multi-class strategy
to train models; an ovr matrix is only constructed from the ovo matrix.
The parameter is ignored for binary classification.

.. versionchanged:: 0.19
decision_function_shape is 'ovr' by default.

.. versionadded:: 0.17
*decision_function_shape='ovr'* is recommended.

.. versionchanged:: 0.17
Deprecated *decision_function_shape='ovo' and None*. 		'ovo'
break_ties break_ties: bool, default=False

If true, ``decision_function_shape='ovr'``, and number of classes > 2,
:term:`predict` will break ties according to the confidence values of
:term:`decision_function`; otherwise the first class among the tied
classes is returned. Please note that breaking ties comes at a
relatively high computational cost compared to a simple predict. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_svm_plot_svm_tie_breaking.py` for an
example of its usage with ``decision_function_shape='ovr'``.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		False
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls the pseudo random number generation for shuffling the data for
probability estimates. Ignored when `probability` is False.
Pass an int for reproducible output across multiple function calls.
See :term:`Glossary `. 		None
In [56]:
clf_rf  = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, max_depth=20, max_features='sqrt',class_weight='balanced', random_state=42)
clf_rf.fit(x_train_120, y_train)
Out[56]:
RandomForestClassifier(class_weight='balanced', max_depth=20, n_estimators=200,
                       random_state=42)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22. 		200
criterion criterion: {"gini", "entropy", "log_loss"}, default="gini"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria are
"gini" for the Gini impurity and "log_loss" and "entropy" both for the
Shannon information gain, see :ref:`tree_mathematical_formulation`.
Note: This parameter is tree-specific. 		'gini'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples. 		20
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions. 		1
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided. 		0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default="sqrt"

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to `"sqrt"`.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features. 		'sqrt'
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes. 		None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19 		0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=True

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree. 		True
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`. 		False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details. 		None
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls both the randomness of the bootstrapping of the samples used
when building trees (if ``bootstrap=True``) and the sampling of the
features to consider when looking for the best split at each node
(if ``max_features < n_features``).
See :term:`Glossary ` for details. 		42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting. 		0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details. 		False
class_weight class_weight: {"balanced", "balanced_subsample"}, dict or list of dicts, default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If not given, all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems, a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example,
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 5}, {0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 1}] instead of
[{1:1}, {2:5}, {3:1}, {4:1}].

The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

The "balanced_subsample" mode is the same as "balanced" except that
weights are computed based on the bootstrap sample for every tree
grown.

For multi-output, the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified. 		'balanced'
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max(round(n_samples * max_samples), 1)` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22 		None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonic increase
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonic decrease

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multiclass classifications (i.e. when `n_classes > 2`),
- multioutput classifications (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- classifications trained on data with missing values.

The constraints hold over the probability of the positive class.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4 		None
In [57]:
print('------------------SVM Classifier metrics------------------')
evaluate_model(clf_svm, x_test_120, y_test)

Zastosowanie Kernel PCA z jądrem RBF nie poprawiło wyników klasyfikacji w porównaniu do klasycznego PCA. W przypadku SVM liniowego oraz Random Forest odnotowano spadek dokładności, co wynika z faktu, że nieliniowa transformacja wprowadzała dodatkową złożoność i szum w danych.

Zadania dodatkowe: Incremental PCA¶

Incremental PCA (IPCA) jest wariantem PCA zaprojektowanym do obsługi danych, które nie mieszczą się w pamięci RAM lub są przesyłane w partiach (np. dane z czujników IoT, logi serwerowe). W przeciwieństwie do klasycznego PCA, IPCA aktualizuje model przyrostowo, co pozwala na efektywne przetwarzanie danych w czasie rzeczywistym. W zadaniu zbadamy, jak: — Wielkość partii (batch size) wpływa na dokładność IPCA — Skalowanie danych oddziałuje na wydajność obu metod — Rozmiar zbioru danych determinuje wybór między PCA a IPCA

  1. Wygeneruj sztuczny zbiór danych przy użyciu funkcji make blobs z biblioteki scikit-learn: 100 000 próbek, 100 cech, 5 skupisk. from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs X, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=100_000, n_features=100, centers=5)

  2. Podziel zbiór danych na treningowy (80%) i testowy (20%)

  3. Znormalizuj dane za pomocą StandardScaler

  4. Oblicz PCA na całym zbiorze treningowym i zmierz czas oraz zużycie pamięci

  5. Zapisz wartość wyjaśnionej wariancji dla pierwszych 10 komponentów

  6. Implementacja Incremental PCA — Przetwarzaj dane treningowe partiami (np. batch size=1000) — Użyj metody partial fit() do aktualizacji modelu — Zmierz łączny czas i pamięć zużytą przez IPCA

  7. Porównanie wyników: — Dla obu metod wyznacz: wariancję wyjaśnioną (explained variance ratio ), błąd rekonstrukcji (MSE) na zbiorze testowym, czas wykonania i zu- życie pamięci — Narysuj wykres porównujący wariancję wyjaśnioną przez PCA i IPCA. — Skomentuj uzyskane wyniki

  8. Analiza skalowalności: — Przeprowadź eksperyment dla różnych rozmiarów zbioru danych (np. 10k, 50k, 100k próbek) — Porównaj czas wykonania PCA i IPCA w zależności od rozmiaru danych

  9. Wizualizacja — Dokonaj rzutowania danych na 2 komponenty główne za pomocą obu metod — Narysuj wykresy rozrzutu i porównaj separację skupisk

  10. Pytania: — Czy wyniki IPCA są zbliżone do PCA pod względem wariancji wyja- śnionej? Uzasadnij. — Jak zmienia się czas wykonania IPCA w zależności od batch size (np. 100 vs. 5000)? — W jakich scenariuszach praktycznych warto użyć IPCA zamiast PCA? — Dlaczego błąd rekonstrukcji może być większy dla IPCA niż dla PCA?

In [89]:
X, y = make_blobs(n_samples=100_000, n_features=100, centers=5, random_state=42)

x_train, x_test = train_test_split(X, train_size=0.8, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
scaler = StandardScaler()
x_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(x_train)
x_test_scaled = scaler.transform(x_test)
In [90]:
from sklearn.decomposition import IncrementalPCA
def train_model(model, X_train, batch_size=None):
    if isinstance(model, IncrementalPCA):
        for start in range(0, X_train.shape[0], batch_size):
            model.partial_fit(X_train[start:start+batch_size])
    else:
        model.fit(X_train)
In [91]:
import time

pca = PCA(n_components=10)


def run_pca():
    train_model(pca, x_train_scaled)

start_time = time.time()
mem_usage = memory_usage(run_pca)
end_time = time.time()

total_time = end_time - start_time
mem_used = max(mem_usage) - min(mem_usage)


x_test_pca = pca.transform(x_test_scaled)
x_test_reconstructed_pca = pca.inverse_transform(x_test_pca)

mse_pca = mean_squared_error(x_test_scaled, x_test_reconstructed_pca)

print(f"PCA - MSE: {mse_pca:.6f} Time: {total_time:.4f} s Memory: {mem_used:.2f} MB")

PCA - MSE: 0.033796 Time: 1.8566 s Memory: 0.00 MB
In [92]:
print(f'{pca.explained_variance_=}')

pca.explained_variance_=array([27.79264065, 25.71166015, 24.61656877, 17.48735475,  0.27970424,
        0.19034983,  0.15762817,  0.13819007,  0.13193155,  0.11974682])
In [93]:
ipca = IncrementalPCA(n_components=10)


def run_ipca():
    train_model(ipca, x_train_scaled, batch_size=1000)

start_time = time.time()
mem_usage = memory_usage(run_ipca)
end_time = time.time()

total_time = end_time - start_time

mem_used = max(mem_usage) - min(mem_usage)

x_test_ipca = ipca.transform(x_test_scaled)
x_test_reconstructed_ipca = ipca.inverse_transform(x_test_ipca)

mse_ipca = mean_squared_error(x_test_scaled, x_test_reconstructed_ipca)
print(f"IPCA - MSE: {mse_ipca} Time: {total_time:.4f} s Memory: {mem_used } MB")

IPCA - MSE: 0.0337998943420774 Time: 4.6048 s Memory: 2.2890625 MB
In [94]:
print(f'{ipca.explained_variance_=}')

ipca.explained_variance_=array([27.79264037, 25.71165982, 24.61656843, 17.48735437,  0.2796719 ,
        0.19027321,  0.15741723,  0.13797117,  0.13149536,  0.11909745])
In [95]:
components = np.arange(1, 11)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.plot(components, pca.explained_variance_ratio_, marker='o', label='PCA')
plt.plot(components, ipca.explained_variance_ratio_, marker='s', label='IPCA')
plt.xlabel('Components')
plt.ylabel('Explained variance ratio')
plt.title('PCA vs Incremental PCA')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
In [78]:
data_sizes = [10_00, 50_000, 100_000]

models = [PCA(n_components=10), IncrementalPCA(n_components=10)]

for ds in data_sizes:
    print(f'----------Data size:{ds}----------')
    for model in models:
        X, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=ds, n_features=100, centers=5, random_state=42)

        x_train, x_test = train_test_split(X, train_size=0.8, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
        scaler = StandardScaler()
        x_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(x_train)

        s_time =  time.time()
        train_model(model, x_train_scaled, batch_size=1000)
        e_time = time.time()

        if isinstance(model, PCA):
            print(f'PCA training time: {(e_time-s_time):.6f}')
        else:
            print(f'IPCA training time: {(e_time-s_time):.6f}')

----------Data size:1000----------
PCA training time: 0.009346
IPCA training time: 0.042920
----------Data size:50000----------
PCA training time: 0.022202
IPCA training time: 2.527379
----------Data size:100000----------
PCA training time: 0.037155
IPCA training time: 3.563741
In [85]:
def plot_2d_projection_simple(X, labels, method_name="PCA"):
    plt.figure()

    plt.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], c=labels, cmap='viridis', alpha=0.7)

    plt.title(f"{method_name} projection")
    plt.xlabel(f"{method_name} 1")
    plt.ylabel(f"{method_name} 2")

    plt.grid(True)
    plt.show()
In [86]:
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_pca_2d = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

plot_2d_projection_simple(X_pca_2d, y, "PCA")


ipca = IncrementalPCA(n_components=2)
X_ipca_2d = ipca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

plot_2d_projection_simple(X_ipca_2d, y, "IPCA")

  1. Wyniki Incremental PCA są zazwyczaj bardzo zbliżone do klasycznego PCA pod względem wariancji wyjaśnionej, szczególnie przy odpowiednio dużym rozmiarze batcha. Wynika to z faktu, że IPCA stanowi przybliżenie PCA i dąży do znalezienia tych samych głównych kierunków wariancji. Niewielkie różnice mogą wynikać z przyrostowego charakteru uczenia oraz zależności od kolejności przetwarzania danych.

  2. Dla małych batchy (np. 100) czas jest większy, ponieważ model musi wykonać więcej iteracji aktualizacji. Dla większych batchy (np. 5000) liczba iteracji maleje, co przyspiesza obliczenia, jednak kosztem większego zużycia pamięci.

  3. Praktyczne zastosowania Incremental PCA:

  • zbiór danych jest bardzo duży i nie mieści się w pamięci RAM,
  • dane napływają strumieniowo (np. dane z czujników IoT, logi systemowe),
  • wymagane jest przetwarzanie danych w czasie rzeczywistym.
  1. Błąd rekonstrukcji w przypadku IPCA może być większy niż dla PCA, ponieważ metoda ta operuje na fragmentach danych i aktualizuje model przyrostowo, zamiast analizować cały zbiór jednocześnie. W rezultacie otrzymywane komponenty główne są przybliżeniem rozwiązania optymalnego, co może prowadzić do większej utraty informacji i wyższego błędu rekonstrukcji.
